title: Umklapp corrections to Landau damping and conditions for non-trivial modifications to quantum critical transport
abstract: We compute the particle--hole bubble for an Ising-nematic metal when the critical Fermi surface approaches the Brillouin zone boundary for $d=2$ dimensions. We find two qualitatively distinct contributions: i)~the standard antipodal piece, which gives $Π_{\rm{ATP}}(\mathbf{q}, iΩ)\proptoΩ/q$ and ii)~an additional umklapp piece from electrons near the zone boundary, which gives $Π_{\rm{U}}(\mathbf{q}, iΩ)\propto Ω^α$ at the minimum umklapp momentum $q\approx Δ_q$ with $α= 2/3 $ or $1/2$ depending on the temperature $T$. At high $T$ when $α= 1/2$, the minimum $T$ for the activation of linear/quasi-linear in $T$ resistivity, which is expected to be $T_U \propto Δ_q^3$ from $z=3$ criticality, could potentially get reduced to $T_U \propto Δ_q^4$ due to the $\sqrtΩ$ term and discuss why we find only one hyper-specific scenario where this possibility might be realized. For $d=3$ the umklapp contribution gives $Π_{\rm{U}}\sim Ω$ irrespective of $T$ therefore $T_U$ is not modified in this case.
summary: This paper examines how Umklapp scattering, occurring when a Fermi surface is near a Brillouin zone boundary, affects the properties of quantum critical metals. It finds that in 2D, these processes introduce new frequency-dependent terms in the boson self-energy, which can alter the temperature scale for the onset of linear resistivity. In contrast, the effect is negligible in 3D. The findings are highly dependent on the specific large-N theoretical framework used to model the system.
main_problem: Investigating how Umklapp scattering corrections modify Landau damping and quantum critical transport in 2D and 3D Ising-nematic metals.
main_result: In 2D, Umklapp processes introduce a new frequency dependence (Omega^2/3 or Omega^1/2) that can potentially reduce the temperature scale for linear resistivity, whereas in 3D, the Umklapp contribution does not modify the standard criticality.
method: The study uses the Random Phase Approximation (RPA) and explicit patch calculations to evaluate the particle-hole bubble and the imaginary part of the boson self-energy.
model_or_system: A 2D and 3D Ising-nematic metal where the Fermi surface approaches the Brillouin zone boundary, modeled via an effective fermion-boson coupling (Yukawa-type) and analyzed in various large-N limits.
key_observables: Landau damping (boson self-energy), resistivity, scattering rate, and the dynamical critical exponent (z).
important_parameters: Momentum transfer (q), frequency (Omega), temperature (T), distance to the zone boundary (Delta q), and the number of species (N).
main_assumptions: Assumes a large-N limit (specifically analyzing naive vs. matrix large-N), uses Taylor expansions for the Fermi distribution and dispersion near the Fermi surface, and assumes Omega is much smaller than the Fermi energy.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows antipodal patches on the Fermi surface; Figure 2 illustrates Umklapp contributions; Figure 3 shows temperature-dependent crossover scales; Figure 5 illustrates the Fermi surface parameterization for scattering rate calculations.
paper_structure: The paper begins by defining the Umklapp contribution to the polarization bubble, proceeds to a dimensional analysis comparing 2D and 3D, evaluates the frequency scaling in different temperature regimes, and concludes by discussing the stability of the z=3 criticality and the relevance of different large-N limits.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. The two antipodal patches contributing to ΠATP.

FIG. 2. The two umklapp contributions to ΠU. O is the center of the original FS. Also shown are the two umklapp partners accessible via momentum transfer q. The proportions are greatly exaggerated for visual clarity. The analysis in the main text focuses on umklapp along the x direction as the steps for the y direction are exactly the same.

FIG. 3. The scattering rate of the electron at Kx F in terms of dimensionless temperature for the SYK model above TNFL. The boson thermal mass has been included here with Tm = T4 set by hand. Here α ≡∆q/kF = 10.0 ≫g2ε−1 F = 1.0 which sets T2 = α−1T3 ≪T3 ≪T4 = αT3. The three tick labels shown on the x-axis correspond to T2, T3 and T4 respectively.

FIG. 4. The same plot for the naive large-N limit. The boson thermal mass has been included in both cases with T2 = Tm for simplicity which leads to √x saturation at large x. Here α ≡∆q/kF = 0.1 ≪g2ε−1 F = 1.0 which sets T4 = αT3 ≪ T3 ≪T2 = Tm = α−1T3. The three tick labels shown on the x-axis correspond to T4, T3 and T2 respectively.

FIG. 5. The parameterization of the FS used to evaluate the scattering rate. The point Kx F corresponds to the electron at θ = 0 which sets θ′ + ϕ = π. For this electron the scattering momenta qϕ ≡q(θ = 0, θ′ = π −ϕ)
